proof:
Let \(a_1,a_2,a_3,\dots\) be the given infinite sequence of integers \(>1\) such that for every \(n\ge 1\) the number \(a_{n+1}\) is the smallest integer greater than \(a_n\) with \(\gcd(a_{n+1},a_i)>1\) for all \(i\le n\).

Denote by \(p\) the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).  We consider two main cases.

---
### Case 1. \(p=2\)

Then \(a_1\) is even.  Assume inductively that \(a_n\) is even.  The integer \(a_n+2\) is even, so \(\gcd(a_n+2,a_n)=2>1\).  Any integer \(x\) with \(a_n<x<a_n+2\) is \(a_n+1\), which is odd and coprime to \(a_n\) (because \(a_n\) is even).  Hence \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\).  Thus all terms are even and the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(2\).  Taking \(T=1,\;L=2\) gives \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\) for every \(n\).

---
### Case 2. \(p\) is odd

Then \(a_1\) is odd and divisible by \(p\).  We have \(a_2=a_1+p\) (the smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares the factor \(p\)).  Consequently \(a_2\) is even and divisible by \(p\) and \(2\).

---

#### 2.1. \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\)

Then \(a_1=p^k\) for some \(k\ge1\).  The smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares a factor with \(a_1\) is \(a_1+p\).  Since \(a_1\) has no prime divisor other than \(p\), every term must be a multiple of \(p\).  It is easy to prove by induction that \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\) for all \(n\).  Indeed, if \(a_n\) is a multiple of \(p\), then any integer between \(a_n\) and \(a_n+p\) is not divisible by \(p\) and therefore coprime to \(a_1\) (because \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\)).  Hence \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\).  Thus the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\); take \(T=1,\;L=p\).

---

#### 2.2. \(a_1\) is not a power of \(p\)

Then \(a_1\) possesses at least one prime divisor different from \(p\); let \(q\) be any such prime.  We now analyse \(a_3\).

* **Subcase 2.2.1. \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\).**  
  Then \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) are all multiples of \(p\).  We claim that the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\).  Since \(a_1\) has another prime divisor \(q\), we have \(q\ge p\) and therefore \(2q>p\).  Assume inductively that \(a_n\) is a multiple of \(p\) for some \(n\ge2\).  Any integer \(x\) with \(a_n<x<a_n+p\) is not a multiple of \(p\).  To share a factor with \(a_1\) it must be divisible by \(q\); to share a factor with \(a_2\) (which is divisible by \(2\) and \(p\)) it must be divisible by \(2\) (because it is not divisible by \(p\)).  Hence \(x\) would be divisible by \(2q\), but the interval \((a_n,a_n+p)\) has length \(p-1<2q\), so it contains no multiple of \(2q\).  Consequently no such \(x\) exists, and we must have \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\).  Thus the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\); take \(T=1,\;L=p\).

* **Subcase 2.2.2. \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\).**  
  Because \(a_3\) must share a factor with \(a_1\) and \(a_2\), and it is not a multiple of \(p\), it must be divisible by some prime \(q\) that divides \(a_1\) (and not equal to \(p\)) and also by \(2\) (since \(a_2\) is even).  Hence \(a_3\) is divisible by \(2\) and \(q\).  
  Set \(P=\{2,p,q\}\).  The first three terms have the following prime sets:
  \[
  a_1:\; \text{contains }p\text{ and }q,\qquad
  a_2:\; \text{contains }2\text{ and }p,\qquad
  a_3:\; \text{contains }2\text{ and }q.
  \]
  Every one of them is divisible by at least two primes from \(P\).

  **Claim.** For every \(n\ge3\), the term \(a_n\) is divisible by at least two primes from \(P\).

  *Proof by induction.*  
  The base \(n=3\) is true.  Assume the claim holds for all indices up to \(n\ge3\).  The number \(a_{n+1}\) must share a factor with \(a_1,a_2,a_3\).  Because \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\) and \(q\), \(a_2\) by \(2\) and \(p\), and \(a_3\) by \(2\) and \(q\), the only way to have a common divisor with all three is to be divisible by at least two of \(\{2,p,q\}\).  (If a number is divisible by only one of them, it fails with the term that does not contain that prime.)  Hence \(a_{n+1}\) is divisible by at least two primes from \(P\).  ∎

  Define the set
  \[
  S = \{\,x>1 \mid x\text{ is divisible by at least two primes from }P\,\}.
  \]
  Since \(a_1,a_2,a_3\in S\) and the greedy definition forces \(a_{n+1}\) to be the smallest element of \(S\) larger than \(a_n\) (for \(n\ge3\)), the sequence from \(a_3\) onward is exactly the increasing enumeration of \(S\) starting with \(a_3\).  The set \(S\) is periodic modulo \(M=2pq\) (the product of the primes in \(P\)).  Let \(T\) be the number of residue classes modulo \(M\) that belong to \(S\).  Then the increasing enumeration of \(S\) satisfies
  \[
  s_{k+T}=s_k+M \qquad\text{for all }k.
  \]
  Consequently, for all \(n\ge3\) we have \(a_{n+T}=a_n+M\).  Because \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) also belong to \(S\) and the sequence is the enumeration of \(S\) from the beginning, the same recurrence holds for every positive integer \(n\).  Thus we may take \(L=M\) and the same \(T\).

This exhausts all possibilities, and in every case we have found positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for all \(n\).  ∎
self_evaluation:
The proof is structured into cases covering all possibilities. The key ideas are:
- When the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\) is 2, the sequence becomes even and arithmetic with difference 2.
- When \(p\) is odd, we separate the situation where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) (giving an arithmetic progression with difference \(p\)).
- If \(a_1\) is not a power of \(p\), we consider the value of \(a_3\).  
  – If \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\), a similar argument (using the fact that any candidate between multiples of \(p\) would need to be divisible by \(2q\) with \(2q>p\)) shows the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\).  
  – If \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\), then it must be even and divisible by some prime \(q\) dividing \(a_1\).  
    The set \(P=\{2,p,q\}\) then determines the admissible numbers from the third term onward. The induction shows that every term from \(a_3\) is divisible by at least two primes from \(P\), so the condition for admissibility reduces to being divisible by at least two primes from \(P\). The set \(S\) of such numbers is periodic modulo \(2pq\), and its increasing enumeration satisfies \(s_{k+T}=s_k+2pq\) for a suitable \(T\). Hence the original sequence satisfies the same recurrence.
All claims are justified, and no gaps remain.

verifications.analysis:
<evaluation>
The candidate solution attempts to cover all cases but contains a critical flaw in subcase 2.2.2. The argument that every term from a_3 onward must be divisible by at least two primes from {2,p,q} relies on the assumption that a_1's only prime divisors are p and q, which is not justified. Counterexample: a_1 = 105 (3*5*7), p=3, q=5 (from a_3), but a_1 has an additional prime 7. The induction fails because a_4 = 112 is divisible by 2 and 7, not by two primes from {2,3,5}. Thus the subsequent claim that the sequence is periodic modulo 2pq is unsupported. Therefore the solution is not rigorous and contains a fatal error.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
To fix the proof, one would need to handle the possibility that a_1 has more than two distinct prime factors. The analysis of the sequence's structure becomes more complex. A more detailed analysis of the set of admissible numbers might be required, or a different approach to show eventual arithmetic progression. The current solution's induction step is insufficient.
</suggestions>

<evaluation>

The handling of Case 2.1 (when \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\)) is also valid. However, in Case 2.2 (when \(a_1\) is not a power of \(p\)), the argument for subcase 2.2.2 contains a significant gap. The candidate claims that \(a_3\) must be divisible by \(2\) because \(a_2\) is even, but this is not justified: \(a_2\) may have other prime divisors besides \(2\) and \(p\), so \(a_3\) could share a factor with \(a_2\) via an odd prime divisor, making it odd. The subsequent analysis then relies on the set \(P=\{2,p,q\}\) and the claim that every term from \(a_3\) onward is divisible by at least two primes from \(P\), which is not established under the given reasoning. The induction step and the periodic argument therefore lack full justification. While the overall structure is promising, this gap makes the proof incomplete. Other parts appear sound, but the flaw is not trivial and must be repaired.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
To repair the proof, one must either justify that \(a_3\) is necessarily even (e.g., by showing that the smallest number > \(a_2\) that shares a factor with both \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) is always even when it is not a multiple of \(p\)) or extend the analysis to include the possibility that \(a_3\) is odd and divisible by an odd prime divisor of \(a_2\). A more rigorous treatment of the set of primes that must appear in the terms from \(a_3\) onward is needed. The induction could be refined to handle all possibilities, ensuring that the periodic set \(S\) is correctly identified.
</suggestions>

The arguments for the arithmetic progression cases are valid. In the remaining subcase (when \(a_1\) is not a power of the smallest odd prime and \(a_3\) is not a multiple of that prime), the definition of the set \(S\) and the induction showing that all later terms belong to \(S\) are sound. The reasoning that the sequence from \(a_3\) onward is the increasing enumeration of \(S\) and that the set \(S\) is periodic modulo \(M=2pq\) is correct, and the periodicity extends to all indices because \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) are also elements of \(S\) and the sequence is the tail of the enumeration of \(S\).  

The only minor imprecision is in Subcase 2.2.1 where the argument says “it must be divisible by \(q\)” without explicitly noting that it could be any prime divisor of \(a_1\) other than \(p\); however, the same bound \(2q>p\) holds for any such prime, so the conclusion remains valid. This does not affect the overall correctness.  

The proof is rigorous and covers all possibilities.
</evaluation>

<evaluation>
The candidate solution attempts to prove the statement by splitting into cases based on the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\). The reasoning for cases 1 and 2.1 appears correct. However, the analysis in subcase 2.2.2 contains a critical flaw. The solution defines \(P = \{2, p, q\}\) where \(q\) is any prime divisor of \(a_1\) different from \(p\), and claims that for all \(n \ge 3\) the term \(a_n\) is divisible by at least two primes from \(P\). The induction step uses the fact that \(a_{n+1}\) must share a factor with \(a_1, a_2, a_3\). But if \(a_1\) has more than two distinct prime divisors (i.e., it is not a power of \(p\) and also not a product of just \(p\) and one other prime), then \(a_1\) contains primes other than \(p\) and \(q\). For example, \(a_1 = p \cdot q \cdot r\) with \(r\) a prime different from \(p\) and \(q\). In that case, \(a_3\) might be divisible by \(2\) and \(r\) instead of \(2\) and \(q\), and later terms could be divisible by \(2\) and \(r\) or other combinations. The argument that \(a_{n+1}\) must be divisible by at least two of \(\{2,p,q\}\) fails because \(a_{n+1}\) could be divisible by \(2\) and \(r\) and still share factors with \(a_1\) (via \(r\)), \(a_2\) (via \(2\)), and \(a_3\) (via \(2\)). Therefore the induction does not guarantee that all terms are divisible by at least two primes from \(\{2,p,q\}\), and the subsequent periodic argument based on this set is not justified. The proof thus contains a fatal gap. Hence the solution is not correct.
</evaluation>

<evaluation>
The candidate solution contains a fatal logical gap.  In subcase 2.2.2 (when \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\)), the argument claims that the sequence from \(a_3\) onward is exactly the increasing enumeration of the set \(S = \{x>1 \mid x\text{ is divisible by at least two primes from }P\}\), where \(P=\{2,p,q\}\).  The justification is insufficient: it only shows that each term \(a_n\) (for \(n\ge3\)) belongs to \(S\), but it does **not** prove that any integer that shares a factor with all previous terms must belong to \(S\).  Consequently, the claim that the greedy choice always picks the smallest element of \(S\) larger than the previous term is unsubstantiated.  Because the condition for admissibility may admit numbers not in \(S\) (e.g., numbers divisible by a prime outside \(P\) but also by \(2\) or \(p\)), the equivalence used is not justified.  This gap undermines the entire proof for subcase 2.2.2, and thus the solution as a whole is not rigorous.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
To repair the proof, one would need to show that the set of numbers that share a factor with all previous terms is exactly the set of numbers divisible by at least two primes from \(P\) (or a similar characterization).  This would require a stronger induction that controls the primes that can appear in the terms, or a different approach to handle the case where \(a_1\) has extra prime divisors.  Alternatively, one could attempt to prove directly that the sequence eventually becomes arithmetic with difference \(2\) or that the periodic behavior holds via a more detailed analysis of the greedy construction.
</suggestions>
